title: Chemistry 2018-2019 (Part 1)
text:
College of the Siskiyous
Program Review: Chemistry
Prepared by Jenny Heath, fall 2018
1. Describe annual progress / changes / events in the program.
College of the Siskiyous (COS) has offered the following chemistry courses (one
section per semester) and plans to offer spring 2019 courses:
Fall 2017: Chemistry 2000
Spring 2018: Chemistry 1000, Chemistry 2100
Fall 2018: Chemistry 1000, Chemistry 2000
Spring 2019: Chemistry 1100, Chemistry 2100
The program saw normal average enrollment numbers for the 2017-2018 year,
however fall 2018 had below average enrollment for Chemistry 2000. The
reason is unknown; however, it should be noted that the course did overlap
with Physics. Because Chemistry 2000 is low enrolled, I would expect to see
low enrollment again in the spring because it is a sequence course. Chemistry
1100 is still on the 3rd semester rotation and is planned for spring 2019. This
has been a low enrolled course since my arrival in fall 2008 and it was noted in
the last program review completed by the former instructor, in spring 2008,
that the course has always been low enrolled, as it is required by allied health
programs outside of nursing. This is also the reason we continue to offer the
course once in a two-year cycle. I would also like to note that Chemistry 2000
was the only course offered in fall 2017 due to the full time instructor being on
leave for part of the semester. The full time instructor will also be taking leave
again this spring.
Chemistry 2929 has not been offered in the last two years but it has been
regularly offered. The program has goals to seek out prospective students to
enroll in the class, in hopes to offer the course at least once every three
semesters.

-- 1 of 4 --

title: Chemistry 2018-2019 (Part 2)
text:
The program continues to work with the Academic Success Center (ASC) to
recommend chemistry tutors and offer a variety of individual and group
tutoring for all sections.
Retention and success rates have been consistent over the past year at slightly
below 90% and 70%, respectively. The program is content with these numbers.
The program continues to host local middle and high school students into its
classrooms and labs for outreach and other campus events. This fall 2018, the
chemistry program hosted the Mt. Shasta Middle School GATE students to
participate in Flame Test Experiments, performed a demonstration hour for the
local Foster Youth students, and welcomed 8th grade students to sit in on
chemistry lectures. Last year the program participated in the First Annual
Science Festival in Mt. Shasta, as well as other outreach events on campus.
Chemistry 1050 (Chemistry in Society), a new non-majors/non-lab course was
designed and approved through curriculum last year. The course needs to be
added into eLumen, as it didn’t get transferred over. The goal is to offer the
course in the 2019-2020 school year.
2. Describe progress on assessing SLOs and PLOs for the program.
Student learning outcomes (SLOs) will continue to be assessed in the same way
they have been in the past. Specific final exam questions will be assessed to
look at the overall success of particular SLOs. Over the course of a three-year
cycle, all of the SLOs for each course (with the exception of Chemistry 1100)
should be completed. Because Chemistry 1100 is offered every third semester,
the cycle to assess all SLOs will take longer. Program learning outcomes
(PLOs) are assessed yearly by multiple assessments including final exam SLOs.
Laboratory equipment and instrument use outcomes are measured based on
the successful completion of a number of lab experiments over the semester
and maintaining a lab with functional instruments. This is analyzed each year

-- 2 of 4 --

title: Chemistry 2018-2019 (Part 3)
text:
to make sure our students are provided with the most updated curriculum,
instrumentation and technology, especially in general chemistry courses.
Curriculum updates have been completed and accepted for Chemistry 2000
and 2100. Updates are planned for the other courses over the next year. One
major change is to reduce the number of SLOs to three per course. This will
allow the program to easily assess all outcomes in a three-year cycle, as
mentioned above.
3. Discuss any support or obstacles encountered by the program.
The chemistry program employs one full-time instructor and shares the
support of a full time classified laboratory technician. As we hire additional
full-time and part-time faculty in the sciences it makes sense to pay close
attention to the workload of the technician. We also depend on the help of
student employees in both Biology and Chemistry, in which they often help out
in others disciplines when time allows. I would like to acknowledge the
support of both of these positions and encourage the District to continue to
grant the resources that allow us to hire student workers and support our full-
time faculty and classified staff.
Presently the program does not offer a local or transfer Associate of Science
degree in Chemistry. The local degree was removed from the catalogue after
the 2015-2016 year, because we do not offer any sophomore level chemistry
courses. I would like explore the idea of offering organic chemistry and
learning more about the ADT in chemistry. It should be noted that offering
organic chemistry will require additional budget monies including instructional
salaries, equipment and instrumentation.

-- 3 of 4 --

title: Chemistry 2018-2019 (Part 4)
text:
4. Describe program budgetary needs or implications. (Submit a CQIP if
you are requesting a budget allocation that falls outside of regular
yearly budget allocations).
All of the information below does not require a CQIP, as the program is not
requesting any specific funding at this time. However, it would like to
comment on a couple of items within the budget.
The waste budget was set up as one total budget that was used to remove all of
the Science Building waste, primarily consisting of chemistry and some biology
but was recently changed to separate budgets for each of those departments.
It best serves the Science Building as one budget line. This also simplifies the
paperwork and payment processes to our current waste removal company,
NRC Environmental, and our laboratory technician.
The chemistry department installed the new PerkinElmer PinAAcle 500, Atomic
Absorption instrument last year. Fall 2018 was the first semester it was used
for student laboratory experiments. We did not originally budget for the cost of
a warranty plan and currently do not have one. Since the instrument is brand
new, the programs feels comfortable not purchasing a plan, as we can
purchase one in the future. It will be the goal of the program to budget and
purchase a plan within the next 5 years as the instrument ages. There is an
upfront cost to purchase the plan which presently would be approximately
$2500, in addition to the yearly cost of the plan.
The chemistry instructional budget is $4600 (not including the waste removal
budget), and the program feels this is an adequate budget to run a rigorous
and successful program.
The last budget item to discuss is professional development funds. Although
these funds are not directly tied to an individual program, I feel that our
current funds are inadequate to allow faculty to travel to conferences related to
our specific discipline or education.

-- 4 of 4 --
